title: Unit A-2 Self-Check (3 questions, for confirming understanding -- no pass/fail record is saved)

questions.q: A physician preparing lecture slides thought, "Image-generation AI works by exactly the same mechanism as text-generation AI, deciding pixels the way it predicts the next word." Which of the following is the most appropriate response to this thought?
choices: That thought is correct: image-generation AI produces pixels one at a time, using the exact same next-word-prediction mechanism that text-generation AI relies on
Image-generation AI starts from an image containing noise and gradually removes that noise to restore it, a different principle from next-word prediction
Image-generation AI converts learned images into a database and simply searches for and combines the ones closest to the instructions
Image-generation AI completes a picture the way a person draws one, adding lines one at a time starting from the outline of each element
questions.explanation: Image-generation AI (a diffusion model) works by starting from an image containing random noise and progressively removing that noise, restoring an image that approximates the given instructions. This is a different principle from text-generation AI (an LLM), which probabilistically predicts the next word based on the preceding context. The claim that it uses the exact same next-word-prediction mechanism (option 1), the claim that it merely converts existing images into a database and searches/combines them (option 3), and the claim that it draws lines one at a time from the outline the way a person does (option 4) are all inconsistent with how the mechanism actually works.

questions.q: A physician who teaches medical education had an image-generation AI create a cross-sectional diagram of the heart. The resulting image looked polished, but on closer inspection, the positions of the atria and ventricles did not match reality. Which of the following, based on how diffusion models work, best explains this phenomenon?
choices: Image-generation AI checks against a medical textbook database each time it runs, so this kind of positional error cannot occur given how the mechanism works
This error occurs only when the prompt is written poorly, and refining the prompt enough prevents it at the level of the mechanism
A diffusion model is not a mechanism that verifies medical accuracy; it merely restores a plausible-looking image close to the instructions, so anatomical errors can occur
This kind of error is a bug limited to early image-generation models and rarely occurs in current models
questions.explanation: A diffusion model is not a mechanism that verifies medical accuracy as it builds an image; it merely removes noise to restore a plausible-looking image close to the instructions. As a result, even a polished-looking diagram can contain anatomical errors, such as organs being positioned incorrectly (the same idea as hallucination in text). The claim that it cross-references a medical textbook database each time, making this error impossible (option 1), the claim that a sufficiently refined prompt prevents the error at the level of the mechanism (option 2), and the claim that this is a bug limited to early models (option 4) are all inconsistent with how the mechanism works.

questions.q: A pharmacist is considering how to use generative AI while preparing slides for a conference presentation. Which of the following is the most appropriate use?
choices: Have the AI draft a slide outline and rough graphs, then personally verify the accuracy of the figures and data against the original source data
Enter patient medication-guidance records as-is and have the AI automatically generate graphs for the presentation without checking the content
Adopt the data and charts the AI produced directly in the presentation material without any checking, on the grounds that the content is too specialized to question
Decide that generative AI produces text and nothing else, and do not use it for the slide outline or for drafting the graphs
questions.explanation: Some generative AI can produce formats besides text, such as a slide outline or a rough graph. That said, you still need to personally verify the accuracy of the figures and data against the original source data. Entering personal information such as patient medication-guidance records as-is without checking the content (option 2), skipping verification because the content is specialized (option 3), and deciding not to use it for the outline or draft graphs on the assumption that it produces text and nothing else (option 4) are all inappropriate.
